answer:
**The closest match in Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation*, Volume I, appears in Chapter 1, where he distinguishes the study of "private morality" (focused on individual actions and habits) from the "political sciences" (encompassing legislation and laws governing nations) as separate domains of inquiry, due to differences in methodology, evidence, and obstacles.**[^1]

He writes: "The obstacles one encounters in the study of **private morality** are less great than those that oppose the formation of the **political sciences**. It is easier to see the causes and calculate the effects of a private action or habit than to see the causes and calculate the effects of **a law that governs a nation**."[^1]

Comte repeatedly pairs "morality and legislation" (or "moral and legislative sciences") as interconnected yet analytically separable branches of knowledge throughout Volume I (e.g., Chapters 1–2), but this paragraph offers the clearest contrast of their domains: morality as proximate, observable individual habits vs. legislation as distant, complex public laws.[^2][^3] No single sentence uses "morality vs. legislation as separate domains" verbatim, but this defines their distinction precisely in terms of scope, certainty, and study.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-d589ad](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-d589ad).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 1](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Chapter 2, paragraph p-338197](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/#p-338197).
